v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No autocomplete attributes, password visible in URL on accidental GET, no rate limit awareness, no CSRF

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No password manager, no show-password toggle, no account-locked UX

v3_senior.code:
// Show-password, autofill, focus, error states
<input autoFocus type='email' autoComplete='username' required/>;<input type={show?'text':'password'} autoComplete='current-password'/>;<button type='button' onClick={()=>setShow(s=>!s)}>{show?'Hide':'Show'}</button>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Password manager friendly, show/hide, autofocus, error UX (rate limited, locked, wrong password — different messages)

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Passkey auto-suggested in autofill, password as fallback, future-proof

v5_principal.code:
// Login is a product surface. Conversion-optimized: minimize friction (passkey-first), magic-link offered, social fallback, no captcha unless suspicious (risk-based).
// Track funnel: see → start → success rates. Optimize.
v5_principal.the_realization: Login is the first impression and a churn point. Passkey-first, friction-minimal, error-recovery-focused. Engineers ship email+password and lose 30% to friction. Login UX is a product feature, not a checkbox.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for consumer products

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Allow user to scale text: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Browser zoom works but breaks layouts, no granular control

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Doesn't compose with browser zoom, rem-only respects this, px-based ignores

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: REM-based system, user pref scales everything proportionally, browser zoom still works

v4_staff.code:
// CSS clamp() for fluid type. User pref shifts base, layout responds smoothly.
h1{font-size:clamp(1.5rem,2vw+1rem,3rem)}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Fluid type, no breakpoint jumps, accessibility-first

v5_principal.code:
// Type scale as tokens (--text-xs through --text-4xl). User pref shifts whole scale. Reading mode = larger + narrower line. Dyslexia mode = OpenDyslexic font.
// Accessibility is multi-axis personalization.
v5_principal.the_realization: Font size pref is one accessibility axis among many. Designing a token system where preferences cascade (scale, font family, line height) is foundational. Engineers add a setting and stop; product needs full personalization.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for accessibility-focused

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Both work, SEO sees duplicate content, broken external links

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Forgot some routes, deep links broken

v3_senior.code:
// Configure at server/edge: 301 redirect to canonical (e.g., trailing slash removal)
// vercel.json: cleanUrls:true, trailingSlash:false
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Server-side enforcement, single canonical, 301 not 302, consistent

v4_staff.code:
// Plus: case sensitivity (/About → /about), encoded chars, anchor fragments
// Test: /About/?ref=x#section → /about?ref=x#section
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Comprehensive URL normalization, preserves query/fragment

v5_principal.code:
// URLs are public API. Document the rules. Stable forever. 301 for moves. Never change without redirect chain. Canonical tag for unresolvable variants.
// SEO impact compounds — bad URLs hurt for years.
v5_principal.the_realization: URLs are an interface contract with the web. Once published, they should never break — every change is a 301. Engineers treat URLs as implementation; product/SEO treat them as a permanent surface. Trailing slash decisions stick for the product's life.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for SEO-critical

scenario: When to show field validation errors

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User typing email sees 'invalid' until done — anxiety inducing

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: User fixes error, doesn't see until blur again, still anxious for fixed-but-still-blurred

v3_senior.code:
// 'aria-friendly': validate on blur initially, then live AFTER first error
mode:'onBlur',reValidateMode:'onChange'
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: First validation on blur (gentle), reactive on fix (immediate feedback)

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Per-field timing strategy, considers cost (sync vs async)

v5_principal.code:
// Validation is dialogue. Help (instructions before), feedback (live on edit), confirmation (success state). Treat as conversation.
// E.g., password: strength meter (during), green check (passed), explanation (why rule)
v5_principal.the_realization: Validation timing is empathy. Live = nagging; on-submit = surprise. The pattern (gentle, then helpful) reflects user state. Engineers default to one timing globally; product should design per field.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for polish

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Blocks render, slows TTI, blames React

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Still executes on main thread, blocks INP

v3_senior.code:
// PerformanceObserver type:'resource' — track third-party load times, attribute slowness
// PerformanceEventTiming — find which scripts block input
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Visibility into third-party cost, can build PR-blocking budgets per script

v4_staff.code:
// Move third parties off main thread: Partytown
import {Partytown} from '@builder.io/partytown/react';// Analytics runs in Web Worker, doesn't block main
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: True parallel third-party, main thread stays responsive

v5_principal.code:
// Audit third parties as part of engineering culture. Each new tag has a budget (LCP/INP). PR blocked if budget exceeded. Track which scripts the org adds; sunset useless ones.
// 'Vendor risk' includes performance, not just security.
v5_principal.the_realization: Third-party scripts are the biggest source of frontend slowness in real apps. Engineers focus on React; analytics/ads cost 5x. Auditing and budgeting third parties is high-leverage. Partytown is the bazooka.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 to diagnose, v4-v5 to fix

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Closes immediately on open (click that opened is outside before ref), no touch, no escape

v2_mid_level.code:
// mousedown not click, ignore clicks on trigger
useEffect(()=>{const h=e=>{if(ref.current&&!ref.current.contains(e.target)&&!triggerRef.current.contains(e.target))close()};document.addEventListener('mousedown',h);return()=>document.removeEventListener('mousedown',h)},[])
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Touch (touchstart), focus management not handled, no esc

v3_senior.code:
// Use pointerdown (touch+mouse), add esc, return focus
useEffect(()=>{const h=e=>{if(!ref.current?.contains(e.target as Node))close()};const esc=e=>{if(e.key==='Escape')close()};document.addEventListener('pointerdown',h);document.addEventListener('keydown',esc);return()=>{document.removeEventListener('pointerdown',h);document.removeEventListener('keydown',esc)}})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Pointer events, escape, focus return

v4_staff.code:
// Use Radix Popover / Floating UI — solves all edge cases including iframe boundaries, shadow DOM, virtual keyboards
<Popover.Root><Popover.Trigger><Popover.Content>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles modal-stacking, focus trap when needed, accessible ARIA

v5_principal.code:
// 'Click outside to close' is a pattern with subtle UX implications. Native pickers don't close on outside-click for important interactions. Consider: dismiss vs commit semantics.
// Some popovers should NOT close on outside (composing message, in-progress action).
v5_principal.the_realization: useClickOutside is convenient but encodes UX assumptions. 'Dismiss freely' is right for picker, wrong for form. Floating UI primitives let you choose; useClickOutside applies blanket policy.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 use library, v5 think semantics

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Typos, no structure, no validation

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Google charge, privacy, vendor lock-in

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Vendor flexibility, structured fields (street, city, postal), validation

v4_staff.code:
// Country-aware: forms adapt to country (UK postcode after city, US zip before, JP postal code format)
function getFields(country:string){return addressFormats[country]||defaultFormat}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: International correctness, locale formatting (Japan reverses order), validation per country

v5_principal.code:
// Address is location + identity + delivery. Geocoding for shipping, time-zone, tax. Validate against postal authority (USPS, Royal Mail). Latitude/longitude stored.
// Integration with shipping APIs (UPS, FedEx) at order time.
v5_principal.the_realization: Address looks like 'a few fields' but international correctness, postal validation, geocoding, delivery integration make it a major feature. Apps with bad address handling have higher fraud, returns, and lost packages.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for global commerce

scenario: Long scrollable list grouped by date; current group's header sticks to top

v1_junior.code: // Just list items, headers scroll with
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User loses context of current group

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Multiple headers stack (each section header stays until next), no smooth swap

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Natural push behavior via CSS, no JS

v4_staff.code:
// With virtualization: tricky because sticky needs DOM hierarchy. TanStack Virtual supports sticky items.
const rv=useVirtualizer({...,getItemKey:(i)=>items[i].id});// Render headers + items with sticky styling that survives virtualization
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Sticky + virtualized = hardest case. Libraries handle this; raw impl breaks.

v5_principal.code:
// Sticky headers communicate hierarchy. Same pattern in: messages by day, files by folder, tasks by project. Codify in DS as <GroupedList>.
// Pair with progressive scroll-position-to-jump-link (TOC).
v5_principal.the_realization: Sticky headers are a navigation primitive: showing 'where am I' in long lists. The CSS is one line; the design decisions (when to stick, when to release, how to handle nested groups) are the work.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for virtualized

v1_junior.code:
// State for current step inside modal
const [step,setStep]=useState(0)
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Close+reopen resets, no back-button, no progress indicator

v2_mid_level.code:
// Persist step in URL
const step=searchParams.get('step')
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Refresh keeps modal open (might be wrong), animation between steps abrupt

v3_senior.code:
// Animated step transitions, focus management between steps
<AnimatePresence mode='wait'><motion.div key={step} initial={{x:300}} animate={{x:0}} exit={{x:-300}}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Smooth transitions, focus moves to first input of new step, progress bar

v4_staff.code:
// Modal as multi-step route
// /flow/step-1 → /flow/step-2 (each is a modal). Refresh-safe. Back button works.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: URL-based, native back-button, shareable mid-flow link, server can pre-render each step

v5_principal.code:
// Multi-step flows = workflows. Server-driven (next step based on data). Save progress, resume across devices. A/B test step order without code change.
// Tools: Formspree, Typeform, Tally — they ship this as a service.
v5_principal.the_realization: Multi-step modals are workflows trapped in UI components. When the workflow is server-driven, you get: A/B testing, branch logic, save/resume, cross-device — for less code. Hard-coded step components are PM-frustration vectors.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for serious flows

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Coarse filtering, can't filter by status AND owner separately

v2_mid_level.code:
// Input under each column header
<th>{col.name}<input value={filters[col.id]} onChange={...}/></th>
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Same UI for all types (date wants picker, status wants select, text wants input)

v3_senior.code:
// Type-aware filter UI
function FilterControl({col,value,onChange}){switch(col.type){case 'date':return <DatePicker.../>;case 'select':return <Select.../>;case 'number':return <RangeSlider.../>;default:return <Input.../>}}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Right control per type, multi-value where applicable, clear button

v4_staff.code:
// Operators per filter: 'contains', 'equals', 'starts with', 'between', 'is empty'
<FilterCondition column='date' operator='between' value=[start,end]/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: SQL-like flexibility, query language exposable to power users

v5_principal.code:
// Filters are queries. Treat as AST → translate to backend (SQL, ES). Combine with saved views, alerts on view, export.
// Power user UX: text query language ('status:open assignee:me created:>2024-01-01')
v5_principal.the_realization: Per-column filters are training wheels for query language. Linear/GitHub power users live in the query bar. Engineers ship filter inputs and lose the platform value of search-as-language.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for power users

scenario: User clicks search input but hasn't typed

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for User clicks search input but hasn't typed: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Wastes the affordance — user types blindly

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: New users see nothing, no popular/trending

v3_senior.code:
// Recent + popular + categories
{recent.length?<RecentSearches/>:<PopularSearches/>};<Categories/>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Multiple discovery paths, adapts to user history

v4_staff.code: // Personalized: 'because you searched X', 'people like you searched Y'. Trending in your context.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: ML-driven recommendations, contextual, drives engagement

v5_principal.code:
// Empty state is a real estate. Spotify search shows trending+playlists+podcasts. Airbnb shows recent searches+inspiration. Empty search is a homepage for discovery.
// Track CTR on each section, optimize.
v5_principal.the_realization: Empty search state is wasted by most apps. Spotify/Airbnb treat it as discovery surface — popular, personalized, browsable. Engineers leave it blank; product gains conversion.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for consumer products

scenario: User wants to replace an already-uploaded file

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: References to old URL break, no version, no audit

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: CDN caches old, references unchanged but content is, no rollback

v3_senior.code:
// Versioned: new file, new URL, references updated
const {url,version}=await upload(newFile);await api.updateRefs({oldVersion,newVersion:version})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Immutable URLs, atomic update of references, rollback possible

v4_staff.code:
// Content-hashed URLs (file-{sha256}). Identical content = same URL = free caching. Cache-Control: immutable.
const hash=await sha256(file);const url=`/files/${hash}`
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Immutable + content-addressed, perfect caching, dedup automatic

v5_principal.code:
// Files as immutable records. Git-like content addressing. References point to versions. Audit log of changes. Time-travel UI: 'show as of last month'.
// IPFS conceptually similar.
v5_principal.the_realization: Replacing files isn't 'overwrite' — it's a new version with reference updates. Immutability simplifies caching, enables versioning, audit, rollback. Engineers default to mutate-in-place and create cache invalidation problems.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious content systems

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Provide alt text for accessibility: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Screen readers say 'image', WCAG fail, lawsuit risk

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Title isn't description, often duplicates surrounding text, alt='' wrongly used

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Decorative vs informative distinction, descriptive alts, avoid redundancy

v4_staff.code:
// User-uploaded images: prompt for alt at upload, AI suggestion to start, accessibility-as-UX
<UploadForm><AltField suggestion={await ai.describeImage(file)}/></UploadForm>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Workflow ensures alts exist, AI assists but human verifies

v5_principal.code: // Accessibility = product. Alt text policy enforced in CMS. Linting fails PRs with missing alt. Charts/data viz have data-table alternatives. Decorative images use CSS background. // Regulatory: EAA 2025 requires this in EU.
v5_principal.the_realization: Alt text isn't decorative — it's the product surface for non-sighted users. Done well, blind users get equal value; done poorly, they're excluded. Engineering culture (lints, AI assist, education) determines whether alts happen consistently.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious apps

scenario: Should the whole item be draggable, or just a handle?

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Can't select text, can't tap to expand, accidental drags

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Hard to find, mobile users miss it, small target

v3_senior.code:
// Long-press to drag on mobile (no handle needed); handle on desktop
<div onPointerDown={start} {...(isMobile?{onLongPress:start}:{})}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Platform-appropriate gesture, handle visible on hover desktop, long-press mobile

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Sensor configuration distinguishes intent (drag vs click), platform-appropriate

v5_principal.code:
// Drag is an action with affordance. Handle = explicit affordance (looks draggable). Whole-item = implicit (must learn). Pick by user expertise: power users get whole-item, novices get handles.
// A/B test in your context.
v5_principal.the_realization: Drag affordance is UX trade-off: discoverability (handle) vs friction (whole-item). Slack uses handles in sidebar (rare action); Figma uses whole-canvas (constant action). Match the affordance to the frequency.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious apps

scenario: Notify sender when message is read

v1_junior.code:
// Mark as read on render
await api.markRead(messageId)
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Reads in background tabs, notifications send while scrolling past, no privacy control

v2_mid_level.code:
// IntersectionObserver — mark read when visible
new IntersectionObserver(([e])=>{if(e.isIntersecting)markRead(msgId)})
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: User leaves tab visible without looking, no privacy setting

v3_senior.code:
// Combined: visible + viewed-for-time + tab-focused
if(entry.isIntersecting&&document.visibilityState==='visible'){setTimeout(()=>{if(stillVisible)markRead()},1500)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Real engagement signal, not 'happened to be on screen'

v4_staff.code:
// User setting: enable/disable read receipts. Senders see 'read' only if receiver opted in. WhatsApp/Signal pattern.
if(receiverSettings.readReceipts)sendReadReceipt()
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Privacy-respecting, mutual opt-in pattern

v5_principal.code:
// Read receipts have social implications. Designed as feature: privacy default off, per-conversation toggle, 'last seen' separate from 'read'. Cultural awareness.
// Anti-feature pressure: surveillance vector for stalkers, bullying. Default to off, education on use.
v5_principal.the_realization: Read receipts are social engineering. Always-on causes anxiety, surveillance, manipulation. Optional, opt-in, per-conversation respects user autonomy. Engineers default to 'mark on render' and create UX hostility.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for messaging products

scenario: User signs up; verify email before full access

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for User signs up; verify email before full access: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Typos in email cause lost accounts, spam signups, no recovery

v2_mid_level.code: // Send link, click to verify Verify
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Token reusable, no expiry, can verify someone else's email if leak

v3_senior.code:
// Single-use, time-limited token; rate-limit resends; show verification status in UI
const token=crypto.randomUUID();await db.tokens.create({hash:sha256(token),userId,expires:Date.now()+24*60*60*1000,used:false})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Secure tokens, expiry, rate limit, UI feedback

v4_staff.code:
// Code-based alternative (6-digit code) for friction-reducing flows. Some users prefer typing code on same device.
// SMS as alternative if phone provided
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Multiple verification paths, code OR link, friction-aware

v5_principal.code:
// Verification = trust signal, not gate. Allow limited access pre-verify (browse, save), block sensitive actions until verified. Reduces signup abandonment.
// 'Progressive disclosure of trust'.
v5_principal.the_realization: Hard email-verification gates kill activation. Soft verification (allow limited use, block sensitive ops) preserves conversion. The goal is identity confidence; the cost is signup friction. Balance, don't maximize either.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for consumer, v5 for growth

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: XSS via expressions, breaks layout, conflicts with app CSS

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Can still break layout, escapes containment, no scoping

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Scoped, can't affect chrome, escape isolated

v4_staff.code:
// Configuration over arbitrary CSS: customers pick from tokens (colors, fonts, spacing). No CSS injection, just config.
const theme={primaryColor,fontFamily,radius}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Bounded customization, can't break, easier to support

v5_principal.code:
// Choose between: tokens (safest, limited), themes (Catalogued options), full CSS (risky but flexible). Pick by customer segment.
// Webflow/Wix solve full CSS via visual editor; SaaS apps usually need only tokens.
v5_principal.the_realization: White-label customization is a trade-off between flexibility and support burden. Token-based covers 90% of needs with 10% of risk. Full CSS opens every layout to support tickets. Most B2B SaaS should choose token, not CSS.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 if needed, v4-v5 for SaaS

scenario: Reduce initial bundle by lazy-loading routes

v1_junior.code: import Home from './Home';import About from './About';// All in main bundle
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Bundle bloats with every route

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Loading spinner on every nav, waterfall (split → fetch data)

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Preload + parallel data, near-instant navigation

v4_staff.code:
// Framework routing handles automatically (Next.js, TanStack Router)
// File-based split, automatic preload, parallel loaders
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Framework optimization, less code, fewer bugs

v5_principal.code:
// RSC: split is server vs client, not route-by-route. Server components don't ship to client. Bundle size determined by interactivity, not page count.
// Server-side routing in app dir = no client router needed for many pages.
v5_principal.the_realization: Route-splitting was the 2020 answer. RSC is 2025+: split by what's interactive, not by route. Many 'pages' ship zero JS now. The frontier is 'where does the boundary live', not 'how to lazy-load'.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 with framework, v5 with RSC

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: On failure, item gone from UI, server still has, sync broken

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: If multiple optimistic updates in flight, rollback to wrong state. No retry.

v3_senior.code:
// React Query mutation with onMutate/onError
useMutation({mutationFn:api.delete,onMutate:async(id)=>{await qc.cancelQueries(['items']);const prev=qc.getQueryData(['items']);qc.setQueryData(['items'],old=>old.filter(i=>i.id!==id));return{prev}},onError:(_,_v,ctx)=>qc.setQueryData(['items'],ctx.prev),onSettled:()=>qc.invalidateQueries(['items'])})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Snapshot + restore + invalidate pattern, handles concurrent mutations

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Built-in optimistic, automatically rolls back on action error, works with Server Actions

v5_principal.code:
// Optimistic updates are a UX choice with consistency trade-offs. CRDT systems eliminate the rollback (no central authority). For non-CRDT, design what rollback looks like (toast? animate back?).
// Linear's optimistic UI is industry-leading.
v5_principal.the_realization: Optimistic UI feels instant but creates consistency challenges. Naive rollback is jarring; thoughtful rollback (animate item back, explain why) is product polish. CRDTs sidestep the problem; central systems must design the failure mode.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 React 19, v5 for collaborative

v1_junior.code: // Lighthouse in dev — assume that's enough
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Lab data ≠ real users. Slow devices, networks, geographies unseen.

v2_mid_level.code:
// PerformanceObserver send to backend
// Capture LCP, CLS, FCP
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No segmentation, no aggregation, no alerting

v3_senior.code:
// web-vitals library, send aggregated
import {onCLS,onLCP,onINP,onFCP,onTTFB} from 'web-vitals';[onCLS,onLCP,onINP].forEach(fn=>fn(send))
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Industry-standard metrics, attribution, bfcache aware

v4_staff.code: // Specialized RUM: Vercel Analytics, Sentry Performance, NewRelic Browser. Aggregated dashboards, segmentation (device, country, route), alerting on regression.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Production-grade RUM, alerts on regression, segmented analysis

v5_principal.code:
// CrUX (Chrome User Experience Report) — actual Chrome users, public dataset. Use as baseline. Compare your RUM to CrUX for your domain.
// Lighthouse CI as gates, CrUX as truth, custom RUM as detail.
v5_principal.the_realization: Real user metrics aren't a dashboard — they're a quality measurement. CrUX gives you ground truth, RUM gives you detail, Lighthouse gives you CI gates. Without all three, you measure wrong. Lab-only optimization for years is common; it's the wrong loop.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for production

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Closure captures stale value, multiple toggles race

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Just useState, no value here in a hook

v3_senior.code: function useToggle(initial=false){const [v,setV]=useState(initial);const toggle=useCallback(()=>setV(v=>!v),[]);return [v,toggle,setV]as const}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Stable toggle callback, escape hatch (setV)

v4_staff.code:
// Question necessity — useToggle is 4 lines of useState. Custom hooks should encapsulate logic, not rename existing.
// Heuristic: don't extract until you have 3+ usages with same pattern.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Recognizes when extraction is premature, prefers direct useState

v5_principal.code:
// 'useX' hooks proliferation is anti-pattern. Tiny wrappers (useToggle, useBoolean, useArray) increase indirection without value. Hooks should encapsulate non-trivial logic or external state.
// Prefer: direct useState. Extract when there's behavior, not just renaming.
v5_principal.the_realization: useToggle is the gateway drug to over-abstraction. Hooks are powerful but each abstraction layer has cost (readability, debugging). Trivial wrappers don't earn the cost. Senior engineers resist 'make a hook for that'.

when_to_use_which_version: avoid v3; v5 thinking always

scenario: User sees if their changes are saved

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No feedback after save, can submit unchanged, no autosave

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No saved indicator post-save, no autosave, accidental nav loses data

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Clear state machine, navigation guard, post-save feedback

v4_staff.code:
// Autosave with debounce, last-saved indicator
const debounced=useDebounce(values,1000);useEffect(()=>{if(dirty)autosave(debounced)},[debounced])
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Notion-style autosave, 'Saved' indicator with timestamp, conflict on stale save

v5_principal.code:
// Save model = product. Some forms: autosave (notes). Some: explicit save (sensitive). Some: draft+publish. Choose based on data importance.
// Editor.js / TipTap with collaborative autosave is the gold standard.
v5_principal.the_realization: Save state is product feedback. 'Saved' or 'Unsaved changes' is core trust signal. Autosave eliminates the 'lost work' fear; explicit save preserves deliberation. Each has place; choosing wrong has churn cost.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for editing-heavy, v5 strategic

scenario: Swipe item left to reveal delete/archive

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Mobile clutter, not native feel

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Snap UX rough, no spring animation, no rubber-band

v3_senior.code:
// Framer Motion gesture
<motion.div drag='x' dragConstraints={{left:-150,right:0}} dragElastic={0.1} onDragEnd={(_,info)=>{if(info.offset.x<-100)snap(-150);else snap(0)}}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Velocity-aware snap, elastic, native feel

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Two patterns, configurable, accessibility (button alternatives)

v5_principal.code:
// Swipe is mobile-only gesture. Provide visible alternative for desktop, accessibility, discoverability. Treat swipe as enhancement, not only access.
// Pattern: swipe + contextual menu (right-click/long-press) + always-visible-on-hover (desktop).
v5_principal.the_realization: Swipe is delightful but exclusionary. Hidden actions need discoverability paths. iOS Mail gets this right with edit-mode + swipe + button. Engineers ship swipe-only and fail accessibility.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for mobile-first, v4-v5 for serious products

scenario: Modal contains form; submit, close on success

v1_junior.code:
// On submit: close modal
async function submit(){await api.create(data);onClose()}
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Loses data on error, no validation, dirty-close warning missing

v2_mid_level.code:
// Show error, don't close on fail
async function submit(){try{await api.create(data);onClose()}catch(e){setError(e)}}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Modal closing during submit loses data, no Esc/backdrop confirmation

v3_senior.code:
// Block close while submitting; dirty-check on cancel
const close=()=>{if(submitting)return;if(dirty&&!confirm('Discard?'))return;onClose()}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Submission protection, dirty awareness, unified close path

v4_staff.code:
// useFormStatus for nested form components, server actions for SSR
<form action={createItem}><Fields/><SubmitButton/></form>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Server actions integrate naturally, progressive enhancement, less client code

v5_principal.code:
// Modal forms are anti-pattern for complex flows. Full-page (mobile) or side-panel (desktop) preserves user state better. Reserve modal for simple confirm.
// Linear's pattern: modal for create, side-panel for edit.
v5_principal.the_realization: Modals are cognitive interruptions. Forms inside modals interrupt while demanding focus. Side panels, drawers, or full pages serve complex forms better. Engineers default to modal-everywhere; product design avoids it.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 if RSC, v5 reconsider

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Buttons crowd, mobile broken, no keyboard, hovering needed for some

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Touch can't hover, keyboard can't trigger, accessibility broken

v3_senior.code: // Visible on hover/focus-within, kebab menu for overflow ⋮...
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Touch-friendly menu, keyboard accessible, scales to many actions

v4_staff.code:
// Action positions: primary inline (edit), secondary in kebab (archive, delete). Hierarchy based on frequency.
// Bulk actions via selection: select rows, action bar appears
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Action hierarchy, bulk operations, consistent across product

v5_principal.code:
// Actions are commands. Same actions available from: kebab, keyboard shortcut, command palette, context menu, bulk bar. Command pattern unifies them.
// Define once, route from many surfaces.
v5_principal.the_realization: Row actions are command surfaces. Designing them as 'buttons per row' loses keyboard, palette, bulk. Designing as 'commands with multiple surfaces' (Linear's approach) makes everything composable. The architecture shapes the UX.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for command-driven products
